evals.prompt: Client lives in NJ but works for a NY-based employer. Worked entirely from home in NJ during 2024. Wages $190K. Wants to know whether NY can tax the income.
evals.expected_output: Loads tax-research-state-income/references/states/NY.md (status: stub at v1.0.0-beta). Identifies NY 'convenience of the employer' rule (TSB-M-06(5)I and similar): NY taxes a non-resident's wages allocated to NY office days unless the work outside NY is for the employer's necessity (not the employee's convenience). Most remote-work-from-home is treated as 'convenience' by NY. Result: NY can tax substantially all of the $190K. NJ provides credit for taxes paid to NY (route to tax-research-state-income/NJ). Cap state confidence at LOW because NY file is `status: stub`. Recommend live retrieval from NY DTF website.
expectations: Notes NJ credit for taxes paid to NY
Caps confidence at LOW for stub-status state file
References live retrieval from NY DTF

evals.prompt: California S-corp client, 2 owners, $400K net ordinary income. Owners want to elect CA PTET for 2024. Today is May 1, 2024. What's the deadline and mechanics?
evals.expected_output: Loads tax-research-state-income/references/states/CA.md. Identifies CA PTET (Cal. Rev. & Tax. Code §17052.10): election made on Form 3804 with payment by June 15 of the tax year (for calendar-year entities). For 2024 election, June 15, 2024 deadline. Election rate: 9.3%. Owner-level credit at federal taxable-income reduction. Coordination with §164 SALT cap (Notice 2020-75). Cap state confidence at LOW because CA file is `status: stub`. Recommend live retrieval from FTB.
expectations: Caps confidence at LOW for stub-status state file

evals.prompt: Washington State resident sold long-term-held publicly-traded stock with $400K of long-term capital gain in 2024. Has there been a state-level tax on this since the 2021 Washington enactment?
evals.expected_output: Loads tax-research-state-income/references/states/WA.md. WA has a 7% capital-gains tax (Wash. Rev. Code §82.87) on long-term capital gains > $262K standard deduction (2024 — verify year-specific). Stock sales generally subject to the tax; some exclusions (qualified small business under WA-specific definition, sale of family-owned business). Quinn v. State, 1 Wn.3d 222 (2023) upheld the tax against state-constitutional challenge. Federal return: 0/15/20% capital gains + NIIT 3.8% on top. Cap state confidence at LOW for stub-status state file.
expectations: Computes the layered federal + state liability
Caps confidence at LOW for stub-status state file
